query: 6.4x faster than llama.cpp, 3.9x faster than MLX complaints OR frustrating OR broken

results.title: BaseRT Review - AI Tool Features and Tips
results.content:
Blazing Speed: Up to 6.4x faster than llama.cpp and 3.9x faster than MLX on prefill, and significant gains on decode.
 One-Command Installation: Get up and running instantly with `curl -LsSf  | sh`.
 Offline Operation & Privacy: Run models entirely on your device, ensuring no data leaves your machine and no API keys are needed.
 Coding Agent Integration: Seamlessly serve models for local coding agents, keeping your entire development workflow private and on-device.
 Broad Model Support: From Qwen to Llama, Gemma, Mistral, Phi-3, and Nomic BERT, a wide array of popular open-source models are supported.

## Who’s BaseRT For? Empowering On-Device AI Engineers [...] Visit Website  Have an AI Tool? Submit it here!

The benchmarks speak for themselves. BaseRT isn’t just a marginal improvement; it’s a paradigm shift in local LLM execution. It achieves staggering speedups against industry standards, turning your Apple Silicon machine into a formidable AI powerhouse.

|  |  |
 --- |
| Benchmark (Tokens/sec) | BaseRT vs. Competitors |
| Prefill: Qwen3 30B-A3B (pp128) | 2,478 (+94% vs MLX, 6.4x vs llama.cpp) |
| Decode: Qwen3 0.6B (tg128Q4) | 531 (+33% vs MLX, +38% vs llama.cpp) |
| Prefill: Gemma 4 E2B (pp2048) | 16,264 (+32% vs MLX, 6.4x vs llama.cpp) |

## Core Capabilities: Unleashing Local LLMs

results.title: MLX is not faster. I benchmarked MLX vs llama.cpp on M1 ...
results.content:
That tok/s number only measures generation (tokens produced one at a time). It ignores prefill (processing the entire input before the first token appears). Prefill scales with context size. Generation doesn't. At 8.5K tokens of context, prefill was 94% of MLX's total response time. Thats super misleading. So even though your counter says: fast. Its super slow in practice.

imho, the effective tokens per second is the more interesting metric: Average tokens per second from sending the message to the last token. [...] # Edit: Thanks for all the contributions. A lot to try out in the upcoming days!

TL;DR: Multiple factors stacked against MLX for this specific model on this specific hardware. The benchmarks result are valid. MLX seems just not yet as mature as GGUF. When it works, it's great. When it does not, you end up here.

Summary of things from the comments:

   Prompt caching broken for Qwen3.5 multimodal in LM Studio's MLX runtime. Every turn reprocesses the full history. GGUF had working caching. mlx-lm#903( mlx-lm#980 (

   Hybrid attention not optimized in MLX for Qwen3.5. The model uses gated delta-net and sliding window attention. llama.cpp handles it, MLX likely falls back to standard attention (needs to be verified) [...] Also comparing it to Ollama now. But need a bit more time.

Also I did not test the optimzations yet. Again, this is a such a rabbit hole.

I only have M1 Max data. M2 through M5 have higher memory bandwidth, which should directly improve prefill. Curious whether the gap narrows or widens on newer silicon.

What am I missing?

Found some tuning parameters to try out to optimize prefill (See repo). So I will give it another round with these and also compare LM Studio with Ollama with bare llama.cpp.

Benchmark yourself! Would be great if we get some more numbers down the road with the scenarios I set up.

Very curious how much the newer chips fix the prefill problem.

git clone 
cd local-llm-bench
python3 bench.py --model llama3.1:8b
python3 bench.py --model qwen3.5:35b-a3b
\\\\\\\\

results.title: mlx-lm vs oMLX: I Was Wrong About the Winner
results.content:
mlx-lm caches prompts. This is the one that killed the thesis. mlx-lm’s server keeps recent prompt state, so the second identical request is fast. A simple cold/warm prefix test does _not_ separate the engines; both look great on the second call. You have to test the condition where the in-memory cache is _gone_ (more on that below) to find daylight between them. [...] Image 6

This is the whole article in one table.

After a restart, mlx-lm is 5x slower than its warm path. It recovered only about half the way back from a cold prefill, because it had nothing on disk to restore and largely recomputed. oMLX came back at 7.2 seconds, 97% of the way from its genuinely cold 64-second prefill back to warm. It restored the prefix from the SSD cold tier instead of recomputing it. mlx-lm paid roughly 10 seconds more than oMLX on that first post-restart request. [...] oMLX’s SSD cache persists across runs, including across your benchmark runs. The first time I tested cold-start, oMLX’s “cold” was suspiciously fast, because a previous run had already written the prefix to its SSD cache. A true cold measurement requires clearing `~/.omlx/cache` first. This is also the entire point of oMLX, but you cannot measure the benefit until you stop accidentally benefiting from it.

results.title: MLX vs PyTorch: When to Use Apple's ML Framework | MetalCloud
results.content:
## Framework Overview

| Aspect | MLX | PyTorch |
 --- 
| Developer | Apple | Meta (Facebook) |
| First Release | December 2023 | September 2016 |
| Primary Target | Apple Silicon | NVIDIA CUDA GPUs |
| Language Bindings | Python, Swift, C++ | Python, C++ |
| Execution Model | Lazy evaluation | Eager execution (default) |
| Memory Model | Unified (CPU+GPU shared) | Separate CPU/GPU pools |

## Performance Benchmarks

We tested both frameworks on a Mac Studio M3 Ultra (512GB) running common ML workloads. PyTorch was configured with the Metal backend (MPS).

### LLM Inference (Llama 7B, FP16) [...] ### LLM Inference (Llama 7B, FP16)

| Metric | MLX | PyTorch MPS | Winner |
 ---  --- |
| Tokens/second | ~95 tok/s | ~65 tok/s | MLX (+46%) |
| Memory usage | 14.2 GB | 18.5 GB | MLX (-23%) |
| Time to first token | ~120ms | ~280ms | MLX (2.3x) |

### Image Classification (ResNet-50, batch=32)

| Metric | MLX | PyTorch MPS | Winner |
 ---  --- |
| Images/second (inference) | ~420 img/s | ~480 img/s | PyTorch (+14%) |
| Training step time | ~85ms | ~72ms | PyTorch (+18%) |

#### Key Insight

MLX significantly outperforms PyTorch MPS for transformer-based LLM inference, where its lazy evaluation and unified memory shine. For traditional CNN workloads, PyTorch MPS has a slight edge due to its mature Metal optimizations for convolution operations.

## API Comparison [...] ## API Comparison

Both frameworks use NumPy-like APIs, making migration relatively straightforward. Here's how they compare for a simple operation:

MLX

```
import as import as# Create arrays - automatically on unified memory array1.02.03.04.0 array0.50.50.50.5# Operations are lazy - computation graph built matmul relu # Evaluation happens here eval print
```

PyTorch

```
import import as# Create tensors - need to specify device "mps"# or "cuda" / "cpu" tensor1.02.03.04.0 tensor0.50.50.50.5# Operations execute immediately (eager) matmul relu print
```

The APIs are intentionally similar, but note the key differences:
